input:
<passage id="p0001" order="1" role="thesis" section_title="S1" span_start="0" span_end="400">
Justice as fairness treats the basic structure of society as the primary subject of justice because its effects are profound and present from the start.
</passage>

<passage id="p0002" order="2" role="objection" section_title="S2" span_start="401" span_end="800">
Critics argue that focusing on institutions neglects interpersonal ethics and the moral quality of day-to-day conduct.
</passage>

<passage id="p0003" order="3" role="reply" section_title="S3" span_start="801" span_end="1200">
A reply is that institutions shape the background expectations within which personal ethics is learned and enforced, so the division is not exclusive.
</passage>

<passage id="p0004" order="4" role="premise" section_title="S4" span_start="1201" span_end="1600">
Stable expectations reduce mutual fear and enable cooperation at scale, which is itself a moral good when fairness is preserved.
</passage>

<passage id="p0005" order="5" role="methodological" section_title="S5" span_start="1601" span_end="2000">
We should therefore evaluate reforms by their long-run tendency to improve the worst-off representative positions, not only short-run efficiency.
</passage>

label.text: Justice as fairness treats the basic structure of society as the primary subject of justice because its effects are profound and present from the start.
